content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission

September 11, 1990


Debra L. Cauble
Sr. Deputy City Attorney
City of San Jose
151 West Mission Street
San Jose, CA  95110

Re:  Your Request for Advice
     Our File No. I-90-427

Dear Ms. Cauble:

     You have requested advice on behalf of San Jose Planning
Commissioner Salvatore Caruso concerning his duties under the
conflict-of-interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the
"Act") pursuant to Regulation 18329(c) (copy enclosed).¹  We are
treating your request as one for informal assistance pursuant to
Regulation 18329(c) because we do not have enough facts about each
governmental decision involved herein to advise him with
certainty.²

QUESTIONS

     1.  May Planning Commissioner Caruso represent applicants in
land use proceedings before the city planning director, or discuss
these matters with city planning department staff on behalf of
applicants?

     2.  Does the answer to Question No. 1 depend on whether a
decision made by the city planning director is appealable to the
planning commission or to the city council?


___________________________
1    Government Code Sections 81000-91015.  All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.  Commission
regulations appear at 2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000, et seq.  All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations.

2    Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section 83114; Regulation 18329(c)(3), copy enclosed.)


428 J Street, Suite 800 ● P.O. Box 807 ● Sacramento CA 95804-0807 ● (916)322-5660

Our File No. I-90-427
Page 2


3. May employees of Planning Commissioner Caruso's
architectural consulting business represent applicants in land use
proceedings before the city planning director or before city
planning department staff?

CONCLUSION

1. The Act prohibits a public official who has a material
financial interest in a decision before his agency from attempting
to use his official position to influence that decision.
Therefore, Commissioner Caruso may not appear before his own
agency (the planning commission) or contact department of city
planning staff (including the director of planning) to represent a
client regarding a matter which is, or may come, before his own
agency. Similarly, Commissioner Caruso cannot represent a client
before the planning director or department of city planning staff
when the decision to be made is one which is appealable to, and
may be reconsidered by, the planning commission. The prohibition
applies because, in both these situations, the decision to be made
is "within or before" the public official's own agency.

2. Generally speaking, a public official can represent the
interests of a client before a city agency not under the budgetary
or appointive control of the official's own agency. This means
that Commissioner Caruso may appear before the director of
planning or his or her staff to represent a client as long as the
following two conditions are met: (1) the decision is one which
is appealable directly to the city council and, (2) Commissioner
Caruso does not purport to act on behalf of the planning
commission.

3. The Act does not prohibit an employee of Commissioner
Caruso's architectural consulting business from representing
applicants before the director or staff of the department of city
planning, before the planning commission, or before the city
council. However, Commissioner Caruso is disqualified from
participating in decisions before the planning commission which
will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial effect
on his architectural consulting business, or on a source of income
to him of $250 or more, or on any other financial interest of the
commissioner.

FACTS

Commissioner Caruso is a design professional who serves on
the city's planning commission. He also is the sole owner of an
architectural consulting business.

In addition to being head of the department of city planning,
the director of planning has multiple functions, powers, and
duties which are enumerated in the city's code. (San Jose
Municipal Code § 2.21.970.) One of the primary responsibilities
of the planning director is to assist the planning commission in
the performance of its functions, powers, and duties. Another is

Our File No. I-90-427
Page 3


to give information and advice to the city council as may be
needed concerning planning and development matters. Another is to
serve as hearing officer and decisionmaker in various land use
proceedings, such as site development permit applications and
tentative subdivision maps. Most of the planning director's
decisions are appealable directly to the planning commission.
However, in some situations, such as the issuance or denial of
sidewalk cafe permits, his decisions are appealable directly to
the city council.

The planning commission has no budgetary or appointive
control over the director of planning or his or her department,
the department of city planning.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits a public official from making,
participating in making or in any way attempting to influence a
governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest. An official has a financial interest in a decision that
will have a foreseeable and material financial effect, different
from the effect on the general public, on the official or on the
official's immediate family or on the following:

(a) Any business entity in which the public
official has a direct or indirect interest worth
one thousand dollars ($1,000) or more.

***

(c) Any source of income, other than gifts
and other than loans by a commercial lending
institution in the regular course of business on
terms available to the public without regard to
official status, aggregating two hundred fifty
dollars ($250) or more in value provided to,
received by or promised to the public official
within 12 months prior to the time when the
decision is made.

(d) Any business entity in which the public
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee,
employee, or holds any position of management.

Section 87103(a), (c), and (d).

Commissioner Caruso is a public official. (Section 82048.)
Therefore, he is disqualified from participating in a decision
that will have a foreseeable and material financial effect on any
of his economic interests, including a client who has paid or
promised to pay him or his architectural consulting business $250
or more within 12 months before the decision. (Sections 87100 and
87103(c).)

Our File No. I-90-427
Page 4


Section 82041 defines "local government agency" as a county,
city, or district of any kind including "any department, division,
bureau, office, board, commission or other agency of the
foregoing." Generally, the Commission has treated different city
departments as different agencies. For example, a planning
commission is a distinct agency from the city council or the
department of city planning. (Stout Advice Letter, No. I-88-313,
copy enclosed.)

An official is disqualified from participating in a decision
which will have a reasonably foreseeable and material financial
effect on a financial interest of the official. Disqualification
means that the official cannot participate in discussions, vote on
a matter, or otherwise try to influence members of his or her own
agency. (Section 87100; Regulation 18700.1; Levinger Advice
Letter, No. I-88-328; West Advice Letter, No. A-88-413, copies
enclosed.)

Prohibited "attempts to influence a decision" are described
in Regulation 18700.1 (below) and include contacting any member,
officer, employee of the official's agency or any agency under the
appointive or budgetary control of the official's agency:

     (a) With regard to a governmental decision
which is within or before an official's agency or
an agency appointed by or subject to the budgetary
control of his or her agency, the official is
attempting to use his or her official position to
influence the decision if, for the purpose of
influencing the decision, the official contacts, or
appears before, or otherwise attempts to influence,
any member, officer, employee or consultant of the
agency. Attempts to influence include, but are not
limited to, appearances or contacts by the official
on behalf of a business entity, client, or
customer.

Regulation 18700.1.

Pursuant to subdivision (a), Commissioner Caruso may not
appear before his own agency (the planning commission) to contact
the department of city planning staff (including the director of
planning) represent a client regarding a matter which is, or may
come, before the planning commission. Appearing before the
planning commission would be an improper attempt to use his
official position to influence a decision. Similarly,
Commissioner Caruso cannot represent a client before the planning
director when the decision is one which is appealable to, and may
be reconsidered by, the planning commission. The prohibition
applies because in both these situations, the decision to be made
is "within or before" the public official's agency.

Nevertheless, because the director of planning and his or her
department of city planning are not under the appointive or
budgetary control of the planning commission, Commissioner Caruso

Our File No. I-90-427
Page 5


may appear before the director of planning to represent a client
provided both of the following conditions are met: (1) the
petition is one which is appealable directly to the city council
and, (2) Commissioner Caruso does not purport to represent the planning
commission includes using official stationery to communicate with
the planning director or any other member, officer, employee or
consultant of the city planning department.

The Act does not prohibit an employee of Commissioner
Caruso's architectural consulting business from representing an
applicant at the city planning department, planning commission,
and city council levels. However, keep in mind that Commissioner
Caruso is disqualified if the decision foreseeably and materially
would affect his architectural consulting business or a source of
income to him of $250 or more or any other financial interest of
the commissioner. (Huguet, Jr. Advice Letter No. I-87-245, copy
enclosed.)

I trust this letter has provided you with guidance regarding
the duties and responsibilities of Planning Commissioner Caruso
under the Act. If you have any further questions regarding these
matters, please feel free to contact me at (916) 322-5901.

Sincerely,

Scott Hallabrin
Acting General Counsel

By: Deanne Stone
Counsel, Legal Division

SH:DS:aa

Enclosures
